learning_outcomes.description: Identify the features of digital interactions and explain how conversations are constructed in digital discourse
Effectively apply theoretical concepts to the data analysis
Carry out a small-scale project on online discourse
Critically examine academic literature on the course content
Effectively communicate the understanding of the language use in digital discourse, both in writing and verbally

assessments.task_description:
In WEEK 2-10 tutorials, students will be engaging with the weekly readings and data analysis in group and individual discussions.

Students will need to prepare for the tutorials by (1) having done the course readings and (2) revisiting the lecture content.

Bringing hard copies of the readings, course materials and your own notes is highly encouraged, as no devices are permitted.

Marking:

There will be 9 opportunities for weekly engagement (1% per week), the best 7 out of 9 will count towards the final mark.

Engagement will be assessed through the contribution to the online document and discussion afterwards.

Each engagement will be marked as follows: 

1 mark - Student shows the excellent engagement with and understanding of the readings and course content

0.5 marks - Student shows some engagement with and understanding of the readings and course content

0 marks - Student does not engage and, thus, shows no to very limited engagement with and understanding of the readings and course content

Generative AI & MT Statement

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
You will need to analyse various datasets as relevant to the course content.

Length: 500 words (excluding references) for written analyses; 5-7 min. for oral analyses

In Weeks 3 and 6, you will have access to the interactional data in the Assessment folder on Blackboard that you will need to analyse in terms of what is covered in the weeks that correspond to the analysis:

 Critical Analysis 1: Weeks 3, 4 & 5 - written submission

Critical Analysis 2: Weeks 6, 7 & 8 - video submission

 In your analysis, you will need to

  • identify linguistic and discourse features in the data relevant to the weeks indicated in the task;
  • explain those features and what happens in the data in terms of the relevant course concepts;
  • support your analysis with the relevant readings from the course (from the reading list). Do not use outside-the-course additional readings.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This is an in-class written analysis of the data that will need to be analysed in terms of the content in Weeks 9 and 10.

Due: Week 11, during the tutorial

  1. Students are allowed to bring the hard copies of the Weeks 9 and 10 readings, course materials and own notes.
  2. Students will have 90 minutes (plus 10 minutes for preparation) to write an analysis of maximum 500 words.

This is a compulsory assessment in the course, meaning that it must be submitted to pass the course.

Generative AI & MT Statement

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.due_date:
Written submission - 9/11/2026 5:00 pm

In-person follow-up discussion - 20/11/2026 5:00 pm

Please note that follow-up discussions will need to be completed by 5pm, Friday EXAM WEEK 2. Students will sign up for a timeslot. The student's assigned timeslot will be considered the due date and time for this assessment.
assessments.task_description:
This multi-stage assessment task is designed so that you could apply your knowledge of the pragmatics of digital discourse to authentic data – a real-life online/digital conversations. This task mirrors real-world practices in the fields such as discourse analysis, pragmatics, conversation analysis, interpersonal and intercultural communication where analysing authentic interaction is essential. 

Project consists of 2 parts:

1) Written submission (Exam Week 1) - 30%;

2) Follow-up discussion (Exam Week 2) - Pass/Fail.

You will need to find a dataset that you would like to analyse. The data needs to be naturally-occurring interactions of digital discourse, including but not limited to such contexts as:

  • emails;
  • chats (e.g. Messenger, WhatsApp, WeChat, Tinder, Bumble, service bot);
  • forums and discussion boards;
  • social media (e.g. Facebook, Twitter);
  • YouTube (videos and comments);
  • Human-computer interaction (e.g. Alexa, Siri, GPS).

! You cannot analyse your own interactions, unless they occurred before taking this course.

IMPORTANT: Start looking for the data early in the semester!

Interaction Project - written submission (35%)

Due: Exam Week 1

Length: 1800-2000 words (excluding extracts and references)

Your written submission must include the following sections and at least 10 relevant references (for more information, see the template on Blackboard):

  • Introduction (explanation of why it is important to research pragmatic phenomena of digital discourse - provide examples from empirical studies);
  • Literature review (research done in relation to the identified phenomenon + aims of this project);
  • Data description (source, participants, context, etc.);
  • Analysis & discussion (here you need to focus on 1 phenomenon from the course that became visible in the recorded interaction): provide in-depth analyses of 2 extracts.
  • Conclusion (present a summary of your research project).

Generative AI & MT Statement

The written task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

Interaction Project follow-up oral discussion (in-person) (Pass/Fail)

Due: Mon - Friday, Exam Week 2

Length: 10 minutes

The Interaction Project follow-up oral discussion is a capstone assessment task and pass/fail hurdle requirement for the course. This means that you must complete it in order to pass the course, regardless of your performance in other assessment tasks.

Students will need to come to an in-person oral discussion of their submitted written Interaction Project, bring a hard copy of their written submission (with notes, if needed) and, if necessary, original publications used in the submission. No devices will be allowed. The oral assessment will take up to 10 minutes, will be audio-recorded and include clarification questions on:

(1) Literature review choice and details of the content of readings summarised;

(2) Data details;

(3) Data analysis claims.

IMPORTANT: If you do not come to the project follow-up discussion or fail to demonstrate your knowledge of the written submission and its completion independent of AI tools, the maximum grade that you can receive for the Course will be a 3.

Generative AI & MT Statement 

Part of this assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
